content.text: in Citations Best Practices is the comparison between using Claude's
(manual quote instructions), where the API feature provides superior quality, validity, and efficiency compared to fragile prompt engineering.
Manual instructions: "First find quotes, print them numbered, then answer with"
"We recommend using the citation feature built into the API instead of a prompt-based approach."
Write complex prompt with formatting rules
"The built-in citation flow is intended to be more reliable than prompt-only citation patterns and can reduce the need for quoted output tokens."

: Claude answers from supplied documents, not parametric memory
2.

: Citations point to specific source spans/pages/blocks
3.

: Plain text = char locations, PDF = page numbers
4.

"The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45]."

Citations metadata:
{
  "cited_text": "The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design.",
  "start_page_number": 45,
  "end_page_number": 45,
  "document_title": "research_paper.pdf"
}

You are an expert research assistant. Here is a document you will answer questions about:

First, find the quotes from the document that are most relevant to answering the question,
and then print them in numbered order. Quotes should be relatively short.

If there are no relevant quotes, write "No relevant quotes" instead.

Then, answer the question, starting with "Answer:".

Do not include or reference quoted content verbatim in the answer.
Don't say "According to Quote  [docs.claude](https://docs.claude.com/en/resources/prompt-library/cite-your-sources)" when answering.
Instead make references to quotes relevant to each section of the answer solely by adding
their bracketed numbers at the end of relevant sentences.

Thus, the format of your overall response should look like what's shown between the
<example></example> tags.

<example>
Quotes:
 [docs.claude](https://docs.claude.com/en/resources/prompt-library/cite-your-sources) "Company X reported revenue of $12 million in 2021."
 [promptlayer](https://www.promptlayer.com/glossary/claude-citations/) "Almost 90% of revenue came from widget sales, with gadget sales making up the remaining 10%."

Answer:
Company X earned $12 million.  [docs.claude](https://docs.claude.com/en/resources/prompt-library/cite-your-sources)
Almost 90% of it was from widget sales.  [promptlayer](https://www.promptlayer.com/glossary/claude-citations/)
</example>

Quotes:
 [docs.claude](https://docs.claude.com/en/resources/prompt-library/cite-your-sources) "Company X reported revenue of $12 million in 2021."
 [promptlayer](https://www.promptlayer.com/glossary/claude-citations/) "Almost 90% of revenue came from widget sales, with gadget sales making up the remaining 10%."

Answer:
Company X earned $12 million.  [docs.claude](https://docs.claude.com/en/resources/prompt-library/cite-your-sources)
Almost 90% of it was from widget sales.  [promptlayer](https://www.promptlayer.com/glossary/claude-citations/)

- Requires verbose prompt with extensive formatting rules
- Must output full quotes (increases tokens)
- Easy to break formatting
- Inconsistent citation quality
- Can hallucinate general knowledge
Text only: "The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45]."
Text + quotes: Must output full quote text + reference
"Prompt-based techniques often require Claude to output full quotes from the source document it intends to cite. This increases output tokens."
Lower: fewer output tokens, no quotes
"Cost savings: If your prompt-based approach asks Claude to output full quotes before answering, that increases output tokens and costs. Citations reduces the need for those quoted output tokens."
"Using the API citation feature will improve citation quality, ensure that all returned citations are valid."
"If you request sources, the model will generate them, but there's a high chance they could be fabricated due to its design, which compels it to produce relevant tokens based on the inquiry."
: page numbers, char indices, block indices
"Returned citations point to specific source spans, page numbers, or content blocks."

"We recommend using the citation feature built into the API instead of a prompt-based approach. Using the API citation feature will improve citation quality, ensure that all returned citations are valid, and potentially reduce the number of needed output tokens."
Understanding the internals of citations vs prompted quotes helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with citations vs prompted quotes has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying citations vs prompted quotes in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Citations vs Prompted Quotes is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
